relationship_to_restored_text: Interpretive contemplative layer aligned by verse. This does not replace the source-audited restored translation.

verses.mystical_translation: Sarai carried the ache of barrenness, and Hagar, an Egyptian enslaved woman, stood inside the household’s imbalance.
Sarai tried to force the promise through Hagar’s body, and Abram listened to her voice.
Sarai took Hagar and gave her to Abram, and Hagar’s agency was swallowed by the household plan.
Hagar conceived, and the power between the women shifted in the eyes of both.
Sarai named the wrong but cast its weight onto Abram, as the harm of the arrangement broke open.
Abram returned Hagar to Sarai’s hand, and Sarai afflicted her until Hagar fled from her face.
In the wilderness, by water, the messenger of the Eternal found the fleeing woman.
Hagar was addressed by name and asked where she came from and where she was going.
The command to return was hard, held within a world where survival and promise were entangled with oppression.
The messenger gave Hagar a seed promise of her own, a future too numerous to count.
Her child would be named Ishmael, because the Eternal heard her affliction.
Ishmael would be wild, untamed, facing conflict yet dwelling before the face of his brothers.
Hagar named the Divine as the God who sees. The enslaved woman became a theologian of being seen.
The well carried the memory of her encounter: the Living One who sees me.
Hagar bore Ishmael, the child whose name remembers that God hears.
Abram was eighty-six when Ishmael was born through Hagar.
